feedback.text: The proposals in the Digital Fairness Act are a very important step toward improving consumer protections, and I believe you can add one additional measure that can protect digital art and video games which in their own right are art of their own. The video games industry is currently the most popular and profitable part of the entertainment industry, affecting millions of consumers worldwide, yet developers and publishers regularly implement unfair practices toward consumers seemingly without any repercussions. Almost all of the points already mentioned in the Digital Fairness Act (addictive design, problematic commercial practices, etc.), are already a precedent in modern online video games and there is also no regulation when it comes to the lifetime of the games being sold to customers. Companies will ask players to pay money upfront to play a game, then after an arbitrary amount of time, they make the game unplayable by shutting down online services. The companies will claim that this is "necessary", but they do not advertise upfront that the product has an expiration date, and they try to avoid paying customers refunds or offer them any way to preserve the product that they paid for. This is very common with so-called "live service" games, where the company claims that because the game is a "service", it can be shut down at any time. The games, however are still being advertised as being "sold" to customers, however, do not conform to any of the requirements of actual services (such as a specific end date for the service at the time of purchase). At minimum, companies which sell "live service" games should be obligated to offer full transparency to their customers about how long their purchase will last, and if support must be shut down ahead of time, then the company should have an end-of-life plan to ensure that customers who bought the product should still be able to access and use the game. Please note, this proposal does not mean that companies have to support a game indefinitely, even if it would result in extra costs and no revenue. The only requirement is that companies should not be allowed to sell a game without an end-of-life plan (to allow customers to continue playing even after the company ends support), or the game has to be advertised with a clear expiration date. The same principles could also be applied to software products in general. Many software companies also use the "service" label to sell products that will not function without connecting to a central server, even though this is not necessary for the purposes of said software. The only reason for this online-only feature is to force users to make regular payments, otherwise the company will shut down their access to the product. Companies who sell such products must provide evidence that their software needs to function in this way, otherwise they are denying customers access to products which they paid for. The below is a very popular issue and the following EU Initiative has - Stop Destroying Videogames (https://eci.ec.europa.eu/045/public/) has already gathered more than 1.3 million signatures which represents the broad interest of such legislation among EU citizens like myself.
